speaker: interviewer
text: This is the beginning of tape 2. You were talking about your family and the fact that you kept chickens for the holidays. Would you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: --like to tell us more of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I've been told between that I used the word [NON-ENGLISH], which means strength, [NON-ENGLISH]. But you also enquired, how could we keep the chicken? Well, we had a metal basin about, I would say, 2, 2 and 1/2 feet in length, and the handles came lower. So my father would put the chicken in the basin and turn the basin upside down and shove it underneath our bed. This allowed air to come through, but it certainly didn't prevent the chicken from clucking and hitting the sides of the basin. And sometimes we'd have a few sleepless nights with all the noise that was being made by the chicken. But that's the way we kept the chicken, until my father had a chance to take him to the [NON-ENGLISH] to have him slaughtered or killed. Now, getting back to my early childhood, I was very sick. For some reason, I had nightmares, horrible nightmares, and they were always the same, of me falling. And there were shapes and sounds, but nothing-- nothing that I could put in-- into any living description. It was just feelings and sounds and shapes. And as a result of that, I was-- I was ill a lot. I had every imaginable illness. And it was decided that I should be singled out to go to camp during the summer. So for about two or three years, the Jewish community allowed me to go to a summer camp in Kreuznach, which is in Germany. It's a very-- what we would call now a resort area. And if you want me to spell it out--

speaker: interviewer
text: Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's K-R-E-U-T-Z-N-A-C-H. In fact, I visited that place when I was an adult, and I went back and I reminisced. But I had some fond memories of that. It was-- I spend six weeks sometimes at that place-- it was a-- like, a children's camp-- and participating in the sports. I do recollect that I once was mischievous. And just before leaving, for some reason I went into the toilet, and I slammed the toilet seat down. And by slamming the toilet seat down, the toilet broke.

speaker: interviewer
text: So of course, they discovered immediately that I was the one responsible for breaking the toilet seat. And I said, you're not-- they told me, you're not going home until your father pays for this. And I was worried. I said, my god, I'm going to be send here-- kept here for life. But fortunately, I was shipped home, and I didn't have to pay-- or my father didn't have to pay for the toilet seat.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I remember, though, that that incident was something that stands out in my memory of Kreuznach. Although there were fond-- I have fond memories of the camp. We did a lot of walking. There was a lot of music. It was a very rural area, picturesque, just like you would see in the most picture postcards of the countryside of Germany, the heart of Germany. And there, of course, you were away from all the tumult, the-- the tumult of the-- of the antisemitism that we experienced in the city, which was a constant thing on a daily basis. But I'll get to that a little later. But that helped me in terms of developing my strength. Also, it-- it motivated me to excel in sports. Because I felt in order to be strong, I had to do a lot of activities, and I excelled in soccer. And I was-- I was very good because I was short. And because I was short, I was very-- also very fast. And we used to play a game where an opponent would try and hit you with a soccer ball. And if he could touch you with the soccer ball without you being caught-- without you catching the soccer ball, you'd be out. And I was able to stand within 3, 4 feet of my opponent who had the soccer ball, and he would take that soccer ball and try and hit me with it. I could duck fast enough to avoid being hit. I was that fast. I prided myself on my ability to-- to be so fast. I'm not sure if that ever served me later in life. But in any event, I knew then that I had some-- some ability in sports, and I excelled in sports throughout my life. But I remember, though, being a goalie once in the game. And the attacker was coming after me, or coming at me, to-- to score a goal. And just at the right moment, he kicked the ball, and the next thing I knew the lights went out. The lights went out, and I saw stars. Then the next thing I realize is somebody had picked me up and putting me on the shoulder, and he say, you saved the goal with your head. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- what-- where did you play at home in Cologne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just in the neighborhood and the school, you know, whatever kindergarten or grade school we had, not organized sports.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of activities did you engage in with your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Now, my little sister Regina, in 1930-- she was born in '37. So in 1938, just before we left, she was only 18 months old. So most of my early youth, there were only four of us, and we paired off. Joe, my older brother Joe, and Fanny used to go together, and Martin and I used to go together. And I was Martin's big brother, and Joe was Fanny's big brother. And we-- of course, I was very close to Martin, very close to Martin, and Joe was very close to Fanny. Although we were in-- in the family close to each other, most of my disagreements or arguments would be with Martin over toys or things that happened. I wouldn't have too much with Joe, except Joe was the domineering person, and he carried that on into adult life. He was the big brother. And big brother, he had to tell you what to do. Of course, you let him do that for a while. But eventually, you know, you get big enough to be able to make your own decisions. But my early recollections in those years was-- was of pairing off-- Martin and I and Joe and Fanny.

speaker: interviewer
text: You started to talk about some of the things that you felt as a Jew in Cologne.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us something about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Antisemitism in Germany is hard for anyone who has not lived through to grasp. But the Germans in Germany had a lot of swear words that they would use that would get under your skin, and they lived with you. And [GERMAN] Jude, you know, [GERMAN], all these things. I mean, at an earliest age, I was exposed to that type of antisemitism. And I knew all the words that they would hurl at you when they saw you. We-- when we traveled in the streets, it was-- if-- you had to be pretty fast, because if any Germans saw you they would immediately start throwing stones at you. You'd become a target. And you learn at an earliest life that when you run from the stones, you don't run in a straight line. You zigzag to avoid being hit. And for some reason, they were able to single you out and identify you as Jewish from a distance. Probably now, in my recollection, it's not because of what you looked like but because of the area in which you live. They knew you were Jewish because maybe you wore different clothing or perhaps you acted differently. But it was-- they knew you were Jewish. And as soon as they saw you, if there were any group of children, they would immediately start picking up stones and start letting you have it. And you had to run fast, as fast as you could, to avoid being hit by a stone. We were not allowed to go in the-- in the-- in the beginning, as my earliest recollections, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they stopped allowing Jews, Jewish people, to go in public parks. And there was a public park in Cologne called Volksgarten, which mean People's Garden, and it had a small pond. And my father once took us there after the Germans had decreed that the Jews could not go, and they found him. They found my father. And they-- two Germans picked him up and were going to throw him in the water and drown him, when some officer came by and says, [GERMAN], you know, let the Jew go. And they just barely let a-- let him alive with the skin of his life. And they had-- they told us, get-- get out of here. But they didn't just say get out. They swore at us until we moved out of there, until we vacated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's the type of lifestyle we had, being-- not being able to mingle with the Germans, having people throw stones at you, not being allowed to go to public parks. We were not allowed to eventually go into German stores. We had to go to Jewish stores. So my earliest recollection in Germany, when I was six and seven, was that it became very intolerable for Jewish people to live in Germany. And I knew that my-- some of my relatives were already making plans to leave. My father had a first cousin who left and came to New York in 1937. Later on in life I saw him, Max Newman.

speaker: interviewee
text: He lived in-- he lived in-- in New York with his family. He was my father's first cousin. In fact, he's the only member of my father's family that I ever knew, that first cousin Max Newman, and he lived in Brooklyn. And he was a waiter in Brooklyn, and he worked in the-- in the same bar as a waiter all his life. And in the '40s when I went to New York, I used to visit him once in a while. So yes, there was one member of my father's family that I knew. Now, Germans-- the Jewish people who could were getting out of there. My father, of course, didn't have the financial ability and didn't know-- didn't have any contacts that he knew of in-- in New York, although on my mother's side there were. So he never made any attempts, as far as I know, to leave Germany. But the-- I do recollect, earlier in my life, a period of time when there was no antisemitism in Cologne. And as best as I can recollect, that was before 1936 when-- when Cologne was part of the occupied territory, or had been occupied by the-- the French and the English after the Armistice of World War I. It was, I think, demilitatized. Hitler walked into the Rhineland in 1936, and nobody stopped him. After he walked into the Rhineland, that's when the antisemitism in Cologne started. So I have some recollections of life-- what life was like at an earlier time when the persecution of the Jews was not as severe as it was after Hitler walked into the Rhineland. And so I had a contrast in terms of what-- what, in my earliest recollections, what it was to live without too much antisemitism and what it was like to live with it on a daily basis. So I-- I wanted to bring that to your attention to show you that, even as a child, I have memories that go back to age three and four in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your parents tell you when things started to get bad?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't have to say anything. You-- you knew. You knew what was going on just from conversations, from listening to the radio. When Hitler talked, they had loudspeakers on every street, and you could hear what he was saying. And so, you know, you-- you could listen to the conversation. And you can hear his virile, I mean, antisemitic tirades on the radio, and it would make you shrink, cringe, the way he talked.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's not something that had to be verbally discussed. Everybody knew. And you didn't have to be an adult. You could be a child and be aware of the hatred. And perhaps that's a kind word. It's more than hatred. It's a virulent antisemitism which causes people to cringe. That type of antisemitism, that the Jew had no significance, no life. He had no value. A dog had a greater value than a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when you have that type of knowledge when you're growing up, it certainly doesn't give you a good self-image. My self-image, if I had any, was from my family, from my father and mother, my self-worth. Thank God for that. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You talked of the violence in the park. Where there any other signs of violence?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was one. Joe was walking right near the-- near a street to where we were living, Alexanderstrasse, and one of the brownshirts was handing out flyers for something. And Joe came by, and the brownshirt asked Joe, who was only 10 years old, to take a flyer. And Joe says, no, I don't want one. And he said, take one. Joe said no. So he pulled out his gun, put it to Joe's head, and pulled the trigger. And it went click. And he says, next time, it'll be a real bullet, take one, to Joe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And another one, he-- Joe mentions-- I was not a part of that-- was he was walking down the street, and somebody had given him a package, says, here's a package. Here's a [GERMAN] mark, one mark. Could you deliver it to such and such a place? And he gave him the address. And Joe, not knowing any better, decided to do-- do a favor for this person. It was an unknown person. No sooner had he taken the package when all of a sudden Joe was accosted by two plainclothes people, official-looking people. Who gave you that package? Where did he ask you to take it? Where did he ask you to take it? How much did he give you to pay it, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And immediately, Joe felt that he was so scared, he blabbed the whole thing out. They took the package from him. And Joe was so mortified. He thought that he was a traitor, that he was supposed to do something in confidence and in secret. And at-- soon as he was accosted, he immediately told everything. Now, he still doesn't know to this day whether or not he got the man in trouble or whether this had anything to do with any activities, whether underground or Jewish. But he felt himself a betrayer. And that is the type of the upbringing that he remembers of what he experienced in Germany. I-- my personal recollections, if I have any more I'm sure they're suppressed. I'm-- I'm sure there-- there were more, but it's hard for me to recall. There are times when I get flashbacks. But on a regular basis, I can't. Maybe it's better that I don't. But I knew, I know, that the-- that there were more incidents, and they were on a regular basis.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember now-- I mean, now just thinking about it, I remember being hit by a stone, actually being hit by a stone. And I was actually mad at myself for not being able to dodge it. I said, how could that happen to me? Why did I let them hit me? I mean, I could see-- actually, I was so good I could see the stones coming in the air. I could actually watch the trajectory, and boom. I was so good. Perhaps that helped me later on in soccer. [LAUGHS] But anyways, I don't have any, right now, personal recollections of specific incidents of persecution and antisemitism, except I did see people, Jewish people, getting beaten up, especially after Crystal Night. I saw that. If-- if you want me to talk about Crystal Night? Yes, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Kristallnacht, as you know, happened in November 1938. Perhaps I should go back and talk a little bit, a month earlier, how my father and Uncle Joe-- Uncle Charles got deported.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, that's important.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- there was some incident that happened, and the Germans used that as an excuse to round up all the Polish nationals living in Germany. And I'm not sure how many there were, anywhere from-- I've heard stories from 15,000 to 25,000 Polish nationals living in Germany, people who had come from Poland after World War I and never became citizens, or were not allowed to be citizens, but were living in Germany. And my father had been living in Germany for a number of years. And they were given an order by the police to report to the police station within two hours to be deported. And the Germans, being efficient people, only wanted the adult males and boys 13 years and older to report to the police station. They figured if they provided the transportation for the adults, the women and children would follow on their own expense.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father was allowed to take one loaf of bread and, I think, the equivalent of 10 marks, which is about, at that time, was maybe $4, $5, maybe not even that much, to-- with him. And my Uncle Herman also was ordered to be deported. And he still has the order of deportation, which I have now-- [INAUDIBLE] had a copy of that-- that ordered them to report to the police station. And all they were given is two hours. So my older brother was only 10, Joe, accompanied my father to the police station. And he begged to go with him, to go with him. And my father said no. And when-- when they got to the police station, he, my father, turned to Joe and said, you're now the head of the family. Make sure everybody gets taken care of. And Joe was only 10 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father and my uncle were sent by a freight train to Germany-- to Poland, and that was the last time I ever saw my father, in Kristallnacht, 1938. And I'm not sure the exact date, November 8, 9, or 10, 1938, and that's my last recollection of ever seeing my father. And after that, of course, once my father and my uncle were deported-- I might just add at this point how Uncle Charles got to London.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: He tells the story now that for three days they were in no man's land between Poland and Germany. And the Germans had kicked out these people, and Poland didn't know what to do with them. So after three days, the Polish government decided to let them in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, before the Germans let them off the train, they had them all line up, all in front of the train. And the Gestapo, with the dogs, German shepherd dogs, just like you've seen in the movies, marched all along the line and asked everyone, everyone, how much money did you bring with you? And if anyone said they brought more than 10 marks, they were beaten on the spot, mercifully beaten on the-- mercifully-- not-- that is mercilessly beaten on the spot with truncheons, and the dogs set upon them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And before leaving, Uncle Charles had two 250-note German marks. He-- he-- what he did is he sewed one into the lining of his clothes, and he sewed one into the lining of my father's clothing. And he knew my father did not have the strength of character that he could stand there and answer the question and lie. And when the Gestapo finally came to my father-- no, first came to Uncle Charles. Uncle Charles says, [GERMAN] mark, only have 10 marks. Then they went on to my father. And my uncle tells the story. He grabbed my father by the hand-- and by the wrist actually, dug his nails into his wrist, squeezed so hard to give him courage. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my father said, 10 marks, and they passed him by.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after that, my father went back to his village in [NON-ENGLISH], and that's where he ended up. And as the story will go later on, there will be discovered what happened. In 1941, when they were rounding-- they rounded up all the Jews of that area and shot them in a mass grave.

speaker: interviewee
text: My Uncle Charles, he had been to London the year before as a visitor in 1937. And while there, he met his future wife. And they started corresponding during that year, and he started learning English. So when he got into Poland, he immediately used his money to go to Danzig, which is now Gdansk, and from there he caught a boat, and he caught a boat to London.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was able to get out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Gdansk-- Danzig was a free port. So with the money, he was able to buy passage to-- to England, and he still had his visa from the year before. And when he got to London, he called, telephoned, Auntie Milly, and Auntie Milly's his wife. And when she came down, they looked at his passport, and they discovered that the visa had only one more day to go. But they arranged for him to stay there, and that's how he was able to get out. And he-- he got his visa extended or whatever he was able to do. And eventually, he joined the British Army during the war. He served in the Commandos, became honorably discharged, lived a long and fruitful life in London, has two children who are alive and well-- alive and well today, Marlene [? Nepler ?] and Richard Gordon, and they have children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father, though, went to his village, and that's where he stayed. He-- I heard later on he found out-- this-- the story will go on-- that we were able to get into Brussels, into Belgium. And he made an attempt to try and join us and from [NON-ENGLISH], from [NON-ENGLISH], and he got as far as the border between Belgium and Germany. And he couldn't go any further, and he was shipped back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, after my father and uncle were deported, Joe tried to see-- now at 10 years old, he's the head of the family-- he's trying to see whether or not he could get us out into-- into Holland. So he takes a train by himself to see if they could get in. He got out of Germany, but in Holland they turned him back. He didn't have the proper paperwork. The Germans, at that time, were willing to let the-- the Jews out of Germany without difficulty. It became a problem for them to come in to the neighboring countries.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after they shipped him back the same day, my mother was able to contact some of her relatives. She had some second cousins in Brussels. And it was arranged that she would take the four oldest children-- not Reggie who was only 18 months old-- take us to the Belgian border. And there she and my cousin from Belgium would board the train and try and talk the conductor into letting us in. So in December-- I'm getting on to the story-- in December, I'm not sure of the exact date, sometime before Christmas, before Hanukkah, my-- we all boarded the train in Cologne, Germany. And my mother went with us to the border, which was Aachen, A-A-C-H-E-N.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then from Aachen, the train went by itself into the Belgian part of the border called [NON-ENGLISH]. And in [NON-ENGLISH], sure enough, my mother's cousin boarded the train and talked the conductor into letting us four children into Belgium. That's how we were able to get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end of tape number 2.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
